behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is a logic error: the module constructs an incomplete request payload. A unit test with a mocked external API can inspect the call arguments to verify all taxonomy labels are included. Mocking doesn't hide this failure—it reveals it by making the request visible for inspection. No real infrastructure (network, OS, non-determinism) is required.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external classification API. Call the module with a test document. Assert that the mock was called with a request payload containing all valid taxonomy labels (e.g., ['legal', 'technical', 'financial', 'other']). This catches failures where the module omits labels from the request.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is missing input validation: the module doesn't validate the API response against the taxonomy. A unit test with a mocked API can return an out-of-taxonomy label and verify the module returns 'unknown'. Mocks can inject any return value, so validation logic is testable without real infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not in the taxonomy (e.g., 'unexpected_category'). Call the classification module. Assert that the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the invalid label. This catches failures where the module lacks response validation.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is inconsistency under real non-determinism. Because the module delegates to an external AI API (which are non-deterministic), the requirement is that repeated calls produce the same result despite the API potentially returning different labels on different runs. To catch a real failure (module doesn't cache or doesn't memoize effectively against a truly non-deterministic API), you must call the real API multiple times. Mocking the API to always return consistent values hides the real failure. Testing against actual API non-determinism requires agentic-level infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Call the module twice with the same document against the real external API (or a test double that simulates non-determinism). Assert that both calls return the same classification label. Run this test multiple times to account for potential API variation. This catches failures where the module doesn't cache results or doesn't handle real API non-determinism, resulting in inconsistent labels for the same document.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: a real, typical document causes token usage to exceed the budget. Mocking the API to report arbitrary token counts cannot expose whether actual documents exceed the budget. To catch this failure, you must send a representative document to the real external API and measure the actual token consumption. This requires a real API call (system-level infrastructure) to validate the constraint against realistic data.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare a representative/typical document that the module is expected to classify. Call the module with this document against the real external API. Assert that the token count reported by the API is ≤ the configured per-call budget (e.g., 2000 tokens). This catches failures where real documents exhaust the budget.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is missing retry logic: the module doesn't detect rate-limit errors or doesn't implement backoff/retry. A unit test can mock the API to return a rate-limit error (HTTP 429) on the first call and success on the second, then verify the module makes two calls and returns the successful result. You can also mock the sleep/wait mechanism to verify backoff is invoked without sleeping in the test. Mocking captures the entire retry path without real network latency.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return HTTP 429 (rate-limit error) on the first call and a successful response on the second call. Mock the retry/sleep mechanism (e.g., time.sleep) to verify it is called with an appropriate backoff duration. Call the module and assert that it makes exactly two API calls and returns the result from the successful call. This catches failures where the module immediately returns an error instead of retrying.
